title: Health & Emergencies
shortSummary: Describe symptoms, visit a doctor or pharmacy, and handle emergencies. Critical vocabulary for travel safety.
culturalNote: Pharmacies ('farmacias') in Spain and Latin America play a bigger role than in English-speaking countries — pharmacists can recommend medicines and even give injections without a prescription for many conditions. Look for the green cross sign. In Spain, the emergency number is 112 (like the EU). In most Latin American countries it's different per service. Carrying your European Health Insurance Card (or travel insurance) is essential.

keyPhrases.english: My head hurts / I have a headache
keyPhrases.context: Doler works like gustar — the body part is the subject

keyPhrases.pronunciation: DOHN-deh ehs-TAH lah far-MAH-see-ah mahs sehr-KAH-nah
keyPhrases.context: Finding a pharmacy for basic remedies

keyPhrases.spanish: ¿Tiene algo para el dolor de estómago?
keyPhrases.english: Do you have anything for a stomachache?
keyPhrases.pronunciation: tee-EH-neh AHL-goh PAH-rah ehl doh-LOHR deh ehs-TOH-mah-goh

grammar.explanation: Doler uses the same structure as gustar: 'me duele' (it hurts me) + body part. The body part is the subject, not you. Use 'duele' for singular, 'duelen' for plural body parts.
grammar.commonMistake: Saying 'yo duelo' instead of 'me duele' — doler works backwards like gustar.

grammar.explanation: Many health conditions use 'tener' (to have): tengo fiebre (fever), tengo tos (cough), tengo gripe (flu), tengo frío (I'm cold). English uses 'to be' but Spanish uses 'to have'.

examples.es: Tengo tos y dolor de garganta
examples.en: I have a cough and sore throat

grammar.commonMistake: Saying 'estoy fiebre' or 'soy enfermo' — most symptoms use 'tener', and 'estar enfermo' is the correct form for being sick.

grammar.explanation: In emergencies, use formal commands (usted): llame (call), vaya (go), traiga (bring). These are subjunctive forms. For informal (tú), it's: llama, ve, trae.
grammar.commonMistake: Using infinitive in commands: '¡llamar al médico!' should be '¡llame al médico!' (formal) or '¡llama al médico!' (informal).

dialogue.setup: A tourist visits a pharmacy in Spain with a stomach problem.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.spanish: Buenos días. Me duele mucho el estómago.
lines.english: Good morning. My stomach really hurts.

lines.speaker: Farmacéutica
lines.english: Since when has it been hurting?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.spanish: Desde ayer por la noche. También tengo náuseas.
lines.english: Since last night. I also feel nauseous.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.spanish: No, no tengo fiebre. Creo que comí algo malo.
lines.english: No, I don't have a fever. I think I ate something bad.

lines.speaker: Farmacéutica
lines.english: Are you allergic to any medication?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.english: No, I don't have any allergies.

lines.speaker: Farmacéutica
lines.spanish: Tome estas pastillas cada ocho horas. Si no mejora, vaya al médico.
lines.english: Take these pills every eight hours. If it doesn't improve, go to the doctor.

quiz.question: How do you say 'My head hurts' in Spanish?
quiz.explanation: 'Doler' works like 'gustar': me duele + the body part that hurts.

quiz.question: How do you say 'I have a fever'?
quiz.explanation: Symptoms use 'tener' (to have): tengo fiebre, tengo tos, tengo gripe.

quiz.question: What does 'embarazada' mean?
quiz.explanation: This is a famous false friend! 'Embarazada' = pregnant, NOT embarrassed.

quiz.question: How do you say 'Call an ambulance!' (formal)?
